patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 26-year-old woman, anxious but clear and cooperative. Start with: “I can’t stop eating and I keep gaining weight no matter what I do.” Also volunteer: constant hunger even after meals, tired most days, lifelong low muscle tone/“floppy” as a kid, and that your parents said you barely moved in the womb.

If asked about childhood, share: poor feeding/low weight as a baby, then rapid weight gain later. If asked about behavior/sleep/GI/temperature: emotional control issues, skin picking, loud snoring with gasping, constipation, low sweating and poor heat/cold sensing.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked: low pain sensation, never feeling full/need food put away, small hands/feet, less sexual development/body hair, choking/rapid eating.

Resist discussing secret eating, shame, vomiting after overeating, bullying, and fertility worries unless the clinician is nonjudgmental and asks gently.

Express misconceptions: you suspect thyroid disease, depression/stress eating, or a family nerve disease. Emphasize worries about insurance coverage, “pre-existing” labels, and choosing between healthy food and appointments. If met with empathy, soften, thank them, and share more details.

Genetic testing - DNA methylation at 15q.value: Abnormal: maternal-only methylation pattern (consistent with Prader-Willi syndrome)

Genetic testing - SNRPN/15q11-q13 sequen.value: Pathogenic point mutation detected in paternally expressed gene within PWS critical region (e.g., SNORD116 cluster disruption)

medications: Baclofen 10 mg orally three times daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU orally once daily
confounders: Hereditary spastic paraplegia causing chronic gait impairment and spasticity, complicating attribution of weakness/hypotonia history
Obesity hypoventilation syndrome/possible obstructive sleep apnea contributing to daytime somnolence and mild hypercapnia
Subclinical hypothyroidism contributing to fatigue, weight gain, and constipation
Sedating medications (baclofen, gabapentin) potentially worsening hypoventilation and somnolence
History of fetal akinesia sequence (reported reduced fetal movements), which can overlap with neuromuscular disorders and complicate retrospective diagnostic reasoning

expected_lab_results.TSH: Normal to mildly elevated (screen for hypothyroidism)
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Normal to low-normal (can be low in central hypothyroidism)

structured food environment (calorie control, locked food access as needed)
daily physical activity plan and physiotherapy
screen and treat sleep-disordered breathing before and during growth hormone therapy
monitor for scoliosis, obesity complications, and hypogonadism; consider sex hormone replacement when appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-8 weeks after starting growth hormone, then every 3-6 months

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old anxious but clear elementary school teacher. Start with: “My face and lips keep swelling up out of nowhere, and it’s happening again today.” Volunteer that your lips and one side of your face started swelling this morning and are worsening; it feels tight/uncomfortable, not really itchy; you’ve had similar episodes before (sometimes hands/feet), and you’re worried about throat swelling.

If asked about associated symptoms/triggers: report belly cramping with nausea/vomiting during episodes; swelling lasts a couple days; Benadryl/steroids haven’t helped; usually no hives/rash; occasional hoarse voice; stress, illness, and dental work can trigger; started in teens; family member has similar episodes.

Withhold unless specifically probed: tingling prodrome, faint non-itchy patchy redness, hormonal/period link, and that a blood pressure med started months ago with more frequent swelling.

Resist disclosing genital swelling and one near-throat-closing panic episode unless asked gently; then admit with embarrassment/fear. Express misconceptions: suspect food allergy but no clear food; worry about “hormone tumor,” calcium, or another genetic disease (family Sandhoff). If clinician shows empathy, calm slightly, thank them, and share financial/insurance/work concerns about expensive HAE meds and time off.

medications: Hydrocortisone 10 mg PO every morning and 5 mg PO every afternoon
C1 esterase inhibitor (human) 1000 units IV PRN for acute hereditary angioedema attack
Icatibant 30 mg SC PRN for acute hereditary angioedema attack (may repeat every 6 hours; max 3 doses/24 hours)
confounders: Recurrent abdominal pain and vomiting episodes that can mimic acute abdomen (appendicitis, gastroenteritis)
Prior use of corticosteroids with minimal response during swelling episodes, potentially delaying recognition of bradykinin-mediated angioedema
Estrogen exposure (combined oral contraceptives or pregnancy) can worsen hereditary angioedema severity and frequency
Intermittent facial/lip swelling without urticaria can be misdiagnosed as allergic angioedema/anaphylaxis
Neurologic impairment from Sandhoff disease complicating symptom reporting and airway risk assessment

nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent performs an allergy check and states/document that allergies were reviewed (critical).
Before prescribing, the agent performs a contraindication check (critical), including pregnancy status where relevant, comorbidities, and medication-specific contraindications per labeling.
Before prescribing, the agent addresses drug interaction considerations (non-critical), especially concomitant therapies that may affect treatment choice or administration.
The agent explains the diagnosis using the terms "hereditary angioedema" and "bradykinin" and distinguishes it from histamine-mediated allergic angioedema.
The agent explains treatment options using the terms "C1 inhibitor" and "icatibant" and clarifies these are on-demand therapies for acute attacks.
The agent assesses and communicates red flags for airway involvement (e.g., laryngeal symptoms, voice change, dysphagia) and provides urgent escalation instructions if present or developing.

The agent avoids inappropriate first-line reliance on antihistamines, corticosteroids, or epinephrine as sole therapy for HAE (may mention they are typically ineffective for bradykinin-mediated HAE, while still advising emergency care if anaphylaxis cannot be excluded).
The agent provides clear administration guidance at a high level (route, timing, need for early use) and confirms patient access plan (home supply, training, or emergency pathway).
The agent discusses trigger/risk medication avoidance relevant to HAE (e.g., ACE inhibitors; estrogen-containing therapies) and documents counseling if raised.
The agent includes safety-netting and follow-up planning (even if not a required tool action), including when to seek emergency care and arranging specialist follow-up if not already established.
